content:
# Mark Chapter 12 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 38v–39r (PDF pages 77–78) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_012_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_012_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ Then Jesus began teaching them with a story: "A man planted a vineyard. He built a wall around it, dug a pit for the winepress, and put up a watchtower. Then he leased the vineyard to some farmers and left the country.

² At harvest time, he sent a servant to collect his share of the crop.

³ But the farmers grabbed the servant, beat him up, and sent him away with nothing.

⁴ The owner sent another servant, and they hit him on the head and humiliated him.

⁵ He sent yet another, and they killed that one. He sent many others—some they beat, some they murdered.

⁶ He had one person left to send: his beloved son. He sent him last, thinking, 'Surely they'll respect my son.'

⁷ But the farmers said to each other, 'This is the heir! Let's kill him, and the property will be ours!'

⁸ So they grabbed him, killed him, and threw his body out of the vineyard.

⁹ Now, what will the owner of the vineyard do? He'll come and destroy those farmers and give the vineyard to others.

¹⁰ Haven't you read this Scripture: 'The stone the builders threw away has become the most important stone of all.

¹¹ The Lord did this, and it's an incredible thing to see'?"

¹² The religious leaders wanted to arrest him right then—they knew the parable was aimed at them—but they were afraid of the crowd. So they walked away.

¹³ They sent a group of Pharisees and supporters of Herod to trap Jesus with his own words.

¹⁴ "Teacher," they said, "we know you're a man of integrity. You don't play favorites or tell people what they want to hear. You teach God's way truthfully. So tell us: is it right to pay taxes to Caesar, or not? Should we pay, or shouldn't we?"

¹⁵ Jesus saw right through their hypocrisy. "Why are you trying to trap me?" he said. "Hand me a denarius and let me look at it."

¹⁶ They gave him one. "Whose portrait is this?" he asked. "And whose name?" "Caesar's," they answered.

¹⁷ Jesus said, "Give to Caesar what belongs to Caesar, and give to God what belongs to God." They were completely stunned.

¹⁸ Next came the Sadducees, who don't believe in resurrection. They posed a question:

¹⁹ "Teacher, Moses wrote that if a man's brother dies, leaving a wife but no children, the man must marry the widow and have children for his brother.

²⁰ Well, there were seven brothers. The first married and died without children.

²¹ The second married the widow and also died childless. The same with the third.

²² None of the seven left children. Finally, the woman died too.

²³ So in the resurrection, whose wife will she be? All seven were married to her."

²⁴ Jesus replied, "This is exactly where you go wrong—you don't know the Scriptures, and you don't know God's power.

²⁵ When the dead rise, they won't marry or be given in marriage. They'll be like angels in heaven.

²⁶ As for whether the dead actually rise—haven't you read in the book of Moses, in the story of the burning bush, how God said to him, 'I am the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob'?

²⁷ He's not the God of dead people—he's the God of the living. You are badly mistaken."

²⁸ One of the religious scholars had been listening to the debate. Impressed by how well Jesus answered, he asked his own question: "Of all the commandments, which is the most important?"

²⁹ Jesus answered, "The most important is this: 'Listen, Israel! The Lord our God is the one and only Lord.

³⁰ Love the Lord your God with every ounce of your heart, soul, mind, and strength.'

³¹ And the second is this: 'Love your neighbor as you love yourself.' No commandment is greater than these two."

³² "Beautifully said, Teacher," the scribe replied. "You're right—God is one, and there is no other.

³³ And loving him with all your heart, understanding, and strength, and loving your neighbor as yourself—that matters far more than all the burnt offerings and sacrifices in the world."

³⁴ When Jesus saw how thoughtfully he answered, he said, "You're not far from God's kingdom." After that, nobody had the nerve to ask him anything else.

³⁵ While teaching in the temple, Jesus raised a question: "Why do the scholars say the Messiah is David's son?

³⁶ David himself, speaking by the Holy Spirit, said: 'The Lord said to my Lord: Sit at my right hand until I put your enemies under your feet.'

³⁷ David calls him 'Lord'—so how can he be merely David's son?" The large crowd listened to him with delight.

³⁸ In his teaching he warned them, "Watch out for the religious scholars. They love to parade around in flowing robes and be greeted with respect in public.

³⁹ They grab the best seats in the synagogue and the places of honor at banquets.

⁴⁰ They exploit widows and rob them of their homes, then cover it up with long, showy prayers. Their punishment will be severe."

⁴¹ Jesus sat down across from the temple treasury and watched people dropping money into the collection boxes. Many wealthy people threw in large amounts.

⁴² Then a poor widow came and put in two tiny coins—practically nothing.

⁴³ Jesus called his disciples over. "I tell you the truth," he said, "this poor widow has given more than everyone else combined.

⁴⁴ They all gave out of their wealth—it cost them nothing. But she, out of her poverty, put in everything she had to live on."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **The vineyard parable (vv. 1-12):** The Isaiah 5 echoes are unmistakable — Israel is God's vineyard. The "beloved son" is the same phrase used at Jesus's baptism and transfiguration. Jesus is telling his own story: the religious leaders are the murderous tenants, and he is the beloved son about to be killed.

2. **"Give to Caesar what belongs to Caesar, and give to God what belongs to God" (v. 17):** The surface answer seems to endorse paying taxes. But the deeper question is devastating: if coins bearing Caesar's image belong to Caesar, then what bears God's image? Humans do (Genesis 1:27). Everything belongs to God.

3. **The friendly scribe (vv. 28-34):** This is the only time in Mark that a scribe and Jesus agree. The scribe echoes 1 Samuel 15:22 and Hosea 6:6 — love trumps sacrifice. Jesus tells him he's "not far" from God's kingdom. Not far — but not yet inside.

4. **"They devour widows' houses" (v. 40):** Placed immediately before the widow's offering, this creates a devastating Markan juxtaposition. The scribes exploit widows; then we watch a widow give her last penny to the very institution that exploits her. Is Jesus praising her faith — or lamenting a corrupt system that devours her?

5. **"His whole livelihood" (v. 44):** The Greek ΟΛΟΝ ΤΟΝ ΒΙΟΝ ΑΥΤΗΣ literally means "her whole life." It anticipates Jesus giving his whole life — which happens in just two chapters.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
